Defendant Mercedes-Benz Usa, Llc Mercedes-Benz And Financial Services, Llcs Motion To Compel Binding Arbitration
Off Calendar Jun-14-2021 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE:
Tsolik Kazandjian, Esq. (858-348-1005; tsolik@rbblawgroup.com) of Rosner, Barry & Babbit, LLP for Plaintiff.
Ali Ameripour, Esq. (ali@thetafirm.com) of Theta Law Firm, LLP for Defendants Mercedes-benz USA, LLC and Mercedes-Benz Financial Services, LLC.

The Court's tentative ruling is sent via email to counsel for review before hearing.
ARGUED (as fully reported in the reporter's transcript). The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANTS MERCEDES-BENZ USA LLC, A LIMITED LIABILITY, MERCEDES-BENZ FINANCIAL SERVICES, LLC's Motion To Compel Binding Arbitration:

The motion to compel arbitration by defendants Mercedes-Benz USA, LLC (MBUSA) and Mercedes-Benz Financial Services, LLC (MBFS) is denied, because arbitration has been waived by 11 months of litigation in this court.
In January 2019, plaintiff Matiana Rodriguez bought a used Mercedes from Mercedes-Benz of San Francisco (MBSF) under MBUSA's "certified pre-owned vehicle" program. Plaintiff alleges MBSF lied to her about the vehicle never having been in an accident, when it in fact had sustained substantial damage. Plaintiff's purchase was via a retail installment sales contract (RISC) that MBSF assigned to MBFS. The RISC had an arbitration provision.
Getting no satisfaction, plaintiff sued on July 21, 2020, serving MBUSA on August 10. Her complaint expressly noted the RISC's arbitration provision and said she was willing to arbitrate. (Id. at 6:3-6.) Days later, a case that MBUSA touts as giving non-signatory manufacturers like it rights to compel arbitration - Felisilda v. FCA US LLC (2020) 53 Cal.App.5th 486 - was published.
However, defendants elected not to arbitrate this case. Instead, the parties embarked on litigation that has consumed more than 60 entries on this court's register of actions. Demurrers, motions to strike, a first amended complaint, an answer, six sets of discovery demands, discovery disputes and case management conferences are but a sampling of activity in the case.
Five of the factors supporting arbitration waiver are present here: (1) defendants' actions have been "inconsistent with the right to arbitrate;" (2) "the litigation machinery has been substantially invoked and the parties were well into preparation of a lawsuit before the party notified the other party of an intent to arbitrate" (3) defendants "delayed for a long period;" (4) "important intervening steps" such as settling the pleadings and discovery have occurred and (5) defendants' delay "prejudiced the opposing party" (and the court) with the expense of eleven months' litigation. (St. Agnes Medical Center v. PacificCare of California (31 Cal.4th 1187, 1196 (internal quote marks omitted).)
Because defendants have waived any rights they had to compel arbitration, plaintiff's other points need not be reached.

Order is signed.

Judge: Richard Ulmer, Clerk: S. Le, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. =(302/RBU)
